name: Korean Beef Bowls
description: Inspired by the traditional flavors of bulgogi, this easy ground beef recipe is the perfect 20-minute dinner.
recipeIngredient: 4 cloves garlic, minced or finely grated
1 teaspoon minced or finely grated peeled ginger
1 tablespoon neutral oil, such as canola or avocado
1/2  medium English cucumber, thinly sliced crosswise (about 1 cup)
1  medium carrot, peeled and shredded (about 1 cup)
1/4  small red or green cabbage, shredded (about 2 cups)
recipeInstructions: Stir 4 minced garlic cloves, 1 teaspoon minced peeled ginger, 3 tablespoons soy sauce, 2 tablespoons granulated sugar, 2 tablespoons water, 2 teaspoons gochujang, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper together in a small bowl.

Heat 1 tablespoon neutral oil in a large 12-inch skillet over medium-high heat until shimmering. Add 1 pound lean ground beef and cook, breaking up the meat into small pieces, until browned, about 6 minutes. Stir in the sauce and cook until it evenly coats the beef, about 1 minute.
Turn off the heat. Stir in 1 tablespoon toasted sesame seeds and 1 teaspoon toasted sesame oil.

Divide 4 cups hot cooked white rice between 4 bowls. Divide the beef over the rice. Divide 1/2 thinly sliced medium English cucumber, 1 peeled and shredded medium carrot, 1/4 shredded small red cabbage, and 1 thinly sliced medium scallion between the bowls.
Make ahead: The sauce can be made 1 day ahead and refrigerated until ready to use.Storage: Refrigerate leftover beef in an airtight container for up to 4 days.
author: Myo Quinn

ai.cliffNotes: Brown ground beef quickly over high heat, coat with a balanced gochujang-soy sauce, then build bowls with rice and fresh raw vegetables for textural contrast.
keyTips: Break beef into small pieces while cooking to maximize surface area and ensure even browning in 6 minutes
Mix sauce ingredients before adding to beef so it coats evenly rather than pooling
Use hot cooked rice and room-temperature vegetables—the temperature contrast is essential to the dish's appeal
Toast the sesame seeds and use toasted sesame oil for deeper, more complex flavor than raw alternatives
